Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine most of the time. I'd just kind of study when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Some weeks I'd put in a decent amount of hours, maybe around 14 or so, but it was never consistent. I'd often start with good intentions at the beginning of the week and then get distracted by other things happening in my life.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, to be honest. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually sit down and focus. If nothing was due, I probably wasn't studying much that week. I know it's not the best approach, but with everything else going on and limited resources at home, it was hard to stick to a schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or one of my classmates messaging me about something we needed to do. Sometimes I'd get a notification and think I should check in. I wasn't one of those people who logged in every day just to see what was new. It was more reactive than proactive, I guess.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The peer support group helped a little because my friends would remind me to check things, so that made me log in more. But on my own, I just never got into a rhythm with it.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually kind of helpful because I could see what other people were thinking and that helped me understand things better. I also liked when there were reading materials I could download because I could look at them later without needing to be online. Videos were okay but they took forever to get through sometimes.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the optional stuff. Like extra readings or supplementary videos—I just didn't have the time or energy for them. Sometimes I'd open something and then close it because it felt overwhelming or I couldn't see how it would help me pass. With limited resources, I had to prioritize what seemed most essential.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or the exam, I'd do it. I needed to know it was going to help me pass, basically. Activities that felt vague or disconnected from what we were being graded on just didn't seem worth the effort. My time is limited so I have to be strategic about what I spend it on.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities had consequences if I didn't do them, so those got my attention. Optional ones just fell to the bottom of the list and usually never got done. It's not that I didn't want to learn more, but when you're stressed and stretched thin, you have to make choices.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and readings and then study them on my phone or print them out when I could. A lot of my studying happened with notes I made myself or stuff I found on YouTube that explained things better. The LMS wouldn't know about any of that, but it was a big part of how I actually learned.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going over my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also study with friends in person—we'd quiz each other or explain concepts. That was real learning but the system would just show me as inactive.
Q11: Oh yeah, all the time. I'd download something once, maybe watch a video once, and then go back to it offline multiple times. My internet isn't always reliable at home, so I learned to grab what I needed and work with it offline. The LMS only saw that first download though.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard. I was stressed about money stuff at home, and I just couldn't focus. My anxiety was pretty bad and I felt like I was falling behind in everything. It became this cycle where I'd avoid the course because looking at how behind I was made me feel worse.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from everything else in my life, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all those incomplete tasks. Confusion about assignments made me put them off too—if I didn't understand what to do, I'd just avoid it. It's not a good coping mechanism but that's what happened.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was just not there. I'd have a video playing but be thinking about a hundred other things. Or I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without absorbing anything. Being logged in doesn't mean I was actually engaged.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd still put it off because I felt overwhelmed or didn't know where to start. The anxiety made it worse—the more I avoided something, the scarier it became. It's like this weird paralysis where you know what you should do but can't make yourself do it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me act. I'd cram everything in the last day or two before something was due. It wasn't healthy and my work probably suffered, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through my avoidance. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have submitted anything.
Q17: When I got decent feedback, it motivated me a little bit. But honestly, sometimes I wouldn't even look at feedback because I was afraid of what it might say. The peer support helped more than instructor feedback—having classmates encourage me meant more somehow. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I wasn't completely failing.
Q18: The course had a lot of flexibility which sounds good but was actually hard for me. I needed more structure to keep me on track. When things were spread out and self-paced, I'd just drift. The weeks where there were clear, specific things due at specific times were easier for me to handle.
Q19: Having so many online courses at once made it really hard to keep track of everything. Twenty courses is a lot to juggle. When the LMS was confusing to navigate or assignments were buried in weird places, I'd just give up looking. Clear organization and fewer clicks to find things would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's trying but struggling to actually complete things. Like they might be reading and watching and trying to understand, but when it comes to putting it all together for an assignment, they freeze up. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff that makes finishing work really hard even when they're putting in effort.
Q21: That's kind of me, actually. I passed even though my LMS activity was pretty low because I did a lot of studying outside the system. My exam score was decent because I crammed and used other resources. The LMS doesn't capture all the ways people learn, especially people like me who work better offline.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you had them open, but they can't show if you were actually thinking or just staring blankly. They miss all the offline work, the conversations with classmates, the mental effort. They also can't show what's going on in your life that affects how you engage.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious and thinking about how things connect. It's not just about logging in or clicking through—it's about your mind being present and wanting to learn. I wasn't always there this semester.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally or how my home situation affected my studies. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that kept me from dropping out, or the offline studying that actually helped me pass. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I was trying—just not in ways the system could see.
